customizationNote: Bengali Hindu funeral traditions are deeply tied to the Sakta and Vaishnav traditions. The ceremonies are conducted in Bengali and Sanskrit.
keyDifferences: White cloth (preferably handloom) is used instead of silk
Special fish offering (Maatsya Tarpan) in some communities
Specific Bengali mantras recited alongside Sanskrit
The Ekodishtha Shraddha on the 11th day is particularly significant

customizationNote: Punjabi Hindu and Sikh families have blended traditions. We accommodate Arya Samaj, Sanatan Dharm, and Sikh-influenced Hindu ceremonies.
keyDifferences: Kirtan (devotional singing) is central throughout the mourning period
Akhand Path (continuous Gurbani recitation) may be included
The 13th day ceremony (Terahvi) is the largest gathering
Langar (community meal) is integral to Punjabi mourning

customizationNote: Tamil Brahmin ceremonies follow the Apasthamba or Bodhayana sutra traditions. Ceremonies are conducted in Tamil and Sanskrit.
keyDifferences: Apasthamba Sutra governs the ritual procedure for most Tamil Brahmins
The shroud is typically white with specific Brahminical markings
Karmandhiga on the 16th day replaces the 13-day timeline for some families
The Ekodhishtha Shraddha is conducted with particular precision on the 12th day

customizationNote: Telugu families follow either Apasthamba or Kaushika sutras. South Indian traditions differ significantly from North Indian ones in cremation procedure.
keyDifferences: Vaikunta Samaradhana on the 11th or 13th day is a large community gathering
Sacred thread is changed during the ceremony
Specific Telugu mantras recited alongside Sanskrit
Masika Shraddha (monthly observances) for one full year is followed

customizationNote: Maharashtrian traditions vary by community — Chitpavan Brahmin, Saraswat Brahmin, and Maratha communities have distinct customs.
keyDifferences: Conch shell (Shankh) is blown differently in Maharashtra at specific moments
The Nagbali Narayan Nagbali is a unique Maharashtrian ritual for unnatural deaths
Ambabai is invoked with specific regional prayers
The 13th day Terahvi may be conducted as a community bhojan (feast) at the family's home

customizationNote: Gujarati Hindu traditions are primarily Vaishnav, though Shaiva communities have different customs. Ceremonies are conducted in Gujarati and Sanskrit.
keyDifferences: Vaishnav families observe simplified rituals according to Pushti Marg
Specific white flower (jasmine) is used in the ceremony
Dwadasa Ahnika (12-day) ceremony is common among Gujarati Brahmin families
The Uttarkriya ceremony includes unique Gujarati elements on the 13th day
